texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We investigate the convergence of symmetric stochastic differential games with interactions via control, where the volatility terms of both idiosyncratic and common noises are controlled. We apply the stochastic maximum principle, following the approach of Laurière and Tangpi, to reduce the convergence analysis to the study of forward-backward propagation of chaos. Under the standard monotonicity conditions, we derive quantitative convergence rates for openloop Nash equilibria of N -player stochastic differential games toward the corresponding mean field equilibrium. As a prerequisite, we also establish the well-posedness of the conditional McKean-Vlasov forward-backward stochastic differential equations by the method of continuation. Moreover, we analyze a specific class of linear-quadratic settings to demonstrate the applicability of our main result.
texts.text: We investigate the convergence of symmetric stochastic differential games with interactions via control, where the volatility terms of both idiosyncratic and common noises are controlled. We apply the stochastic maximum principle, following the approach of Laurière and Tangpi, to reduce the convergence analysis to the study of forward-backward propagation of chaos. Under the standard monotonicity conditions, we derive quantitative convergence rates for openloop Nash equilibria of N -player stochastic differential games toward the corresponding mean field equilibrium. As a prerequisite, we also establish the well-posedness of the conditional McKean-Vlasov forward-backward stochastic differential equations by the method of continuation. Moreover, we analyze a specific class of linear-quadratic settings to demonstrate the applicability of our main result.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.1 Literature review
texts.text: 1.1 Literature review

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Introduced by Lasry and Lions [34], and Huang et al. [28], MFGs have become a powerful framework for modeling large systems of interacting agents. This framework considers a system of a large number of homogeneous agents who optimize their individual strategies while interacting weakly with the rest of the population that is, each player responds to the empirical distribution of the entire population. The theory analyzes the limiting behavior as the population size N →∞ . As a characterization of the MFE, a coupled system of the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equation and Fokker-Planck equation was introduced in [34]. The former characterizes the optimality of the representative agent, while the latter describes the flow of the distribution of the player's state.
texts.text: Introduced by Lasry and Lions [34], and Huang et al. [28], MFGs have become a powerful framework for modeling large systems of interacting agents. This framework considers a system of a large number of homogeneous agents who optimize their individual strategies while interacting weakly with the rest of the population that is, each player responds to the empirical distribution of the entire population. The theory analyzes the limiting behavior as the population size N →∞ . As a characterization of the MFE, a coupled system of the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equation and Fokker-Planck equation was introduced in [34]. The former characterizes the optimality of the representative agent, while the latter describes the flow of the distribution of the player's state.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Diverging from the compactness approach, the seminal work by Cardaliaguet et al. [10] succeeded in quantifying the convergence rate for closed-loop controls. By characterizing the value functions in the MFE via the master equation-which is, formally, the limit of the N -Nash system-they derived the convergence rate of the error between the master equation's solution and the value functions of the N -player game. This approach was further applied in [2, 3, 4, 5, 17, 18]. Remarkably, all of these papers also addressed the case with (uncontrolled) common noise.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since the advent of the theory, the first direction of the convergence problem has been actively investigated: specifically, how the optimal strategy obtained in an MFE can form an ε -Nash equilibrium for an N -player game. However, the other direction-the naturally arising question of how and in what sense NE converge to the corresponding MFE-remained open for some time during the first decade of research in MFGs. The first major breakthroughs were achieved by Fischer [21] and Lacker [32], who established the convergence of the empirical measures associated with the NE using compactness arguments. Lacker [33] subsequently generalized these results to closed-loop controls, and Djete [20] extended them to accommodate extended MFGs.

texts.orig: However, none of the aforementioned works regarding convergence rates address MFGs with interactions through controls. Notably, such interactions naturally arise in many problems in finance and economics. See, for instance, [1, 11, 15, 25, 26] and [14, Sections 1.3.2 and 1.4.4] for specific applications. For general studies of extended MFGs, we refer to [9, 14, 23, 24] and [12, Section 4.6].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Laurière and Tangpi [36] were the first to derive the convergence rates for these extended games. They characterized the equilibrium via FBSDEs and generalized the propagation of chaos technique to coupled forward-backward systems. This technique originates from Sznitman [40] and was adapted to backward equations in [35]. Possamaï and Tangpi [38] followed a similar path for the non-Markovian setting using the weak formulation [14]. Instead of the SMP, they employed the dynamic programming principle to characterize the MFE via generalized MKV-BSDEs and obtained convergence rates for closed-loop equilibria. However, both approaches have a limitation. To establish convergence for an arbitrary time horizon T > 0, the drift coefficient b must satisfy a dissipativity condition with a sufficiently large constant K B (see [36, Theorem 2.2] and [38, Theorem 2.10]). Furthermore, neither work covers the case of controlled volatility.
texts.text: Laurière and Tangpi [36] were the first to derive the convergence rates for these extended games. They characterized the equilibrium via FBSDEs and generalized the propagation of chaos technique to coupled forward-backward systems. This technique originates from Sznitman [40] and was adapted to backward equations in [35]. Possamaï and Tangpi [38] followed a similar path for the non-Markovian setting using the weak formulation [14]. Instead of the SMP, they employed the dynamic programming principle to characterize the MFE via generalized MKV-BSDEs and obtained convergence rates for closed-loop equilibria. However, both approaches have a limitation. To establish convergence for an arbitrary time horizon T > 0, the drift coefficient b must satisfy a dissipativity condition with a sufficiently large constant K B (see [36, Theorem 2.2] and [38, Theorem 2.10]). Furthermore, neither work covers the case of controlled volatility.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Up to now, the study of MFGs with interaction through controls, and volatility control has been limited, with the notable exception of [20]. Incorporating these features simultaneously increases the difficulty of the problem significantly. In the analytic approaches, volatility control typically complicates the Hamiltonian of the HJB equations, often resulting in the loss of semilinearity. Furthermore, in the coupled PDE framework [34], the dependence on the control distribution strengthens the coupling as in [26]. In the FBSDE approach, a major challenge lies in the regularity of the flow of distributions ( L 1 ( X t , α t )) t ∈ [0 ,T ] . Without volatility control, this flow is usually continuous. This allows for the use of Schauder's fixed point argument on the space C ([0 , T ]; P 2 ( R n × R n )) to guarantee the existence of MKV-FBSDEs, as studied in [12, Section 4.6]. The difficulty arising from the irregularity of the flow (or more precisely, the flow of the distribution of the randomized control) was also highlighted in [19, Remark 2.5] specifically for mean field control (MFC) problems. Djete [20] addressed this issue using measure-valued control rules, inspired by the relaxed controls introduced in [32], and obtained convergence results for extended MFGs with volatility control via compactness arguments under general assumptions. However, the volatility of the common noise was not controlled, nor was a rate of convergence provided.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recently, Jackson and Tangpi [30] made significant progress on these convergence results. Building on the probabilistic framework of [36], they quantified convergence for arbitrary time horizons T > 0, covering settings with controlled volatility and common noise. Key to their approach was the displacement monotonicity condition. This allowed them to dispense with the smallness condition on K b required in [36, 38]. Using this condition, they applied the method of continuation to guarantee the well-posedness of the conditional MKVFBSDEs and, consequently, the unique existence of the MFE. However, their analysis remains restricted to classical MFGs.

texts.orig: In this paper, we establish the convergence rate for extended MFGs in which the volatilities of both idiosyncratic and common noises are controlled. Crucial to achieving this goal is the monotonicity condition (Assumption 2.12). Various monotonicity conditions have been widely studied in the context of the method of continuation to prove the well-posedness of (MKV)-FBSDEs, as seen in [8, 27, 37], [41, Chapter 8.4], and [30, Appendix A]. As previously mentioned, Jackson and Tangpi [30] investigated several types of (displacement) monotonicity conditions for the convergence problem of classical MFGs.

texts.orig: Recall that our primary goal is establishing an estimate for the error | α N,i -α i ∣ ∣ 2 . In the N -player game, the optimal strategy α N,i t of the player i depends on its own state, the empirical distribution of all the players' states, plus an additional term involving the other players' actions (denoted by ζ ; see (4.1)). This term, which is specific to the extended setting, complicates the error estimation. In contrast to [36], which addressed this term by assuming a small time horizon T or a dissipativity condition, our monotonicity condition also plays a crucial role in this estimate. Consequently, under an additional smoothness condition on the coefficients with respect to the measure arguments (Assumption 2.13), we obtain the quantitative convergence rate using the propagation of chaos technique.

texts.orig: In our context, the primary advantage of the monotonicity condition-similar to the one in [8, (A1)]-is that it circumvents the need for flow regularity. This enables proving the well-posedness of the MKV-FBSDEs via Banach's fixed point theorem on the space of square-integrable processes, avoiding reliance on Schauder's fixed point theorem on the space of measure flows, and thus we can establish the unique existence of the MFE for extended MFGs in a strong formulation.

For example, Graber [25] analyzed extended LQ-MFC problems and LQ-MFGs. He derived the unique existence of an MFE by restricting the analysis to cases where the state coefficients lack distributional dependence on either the state or the control.

texts.orig: This restriction ensures the equivalence of the MFC and MFG problems. Bensoussan et al. [9] addressed extended MFGs without volatility control. To obtain an MFE, they analyzed the well-posedness of linear FBODEs, derived by taking expectations of the original MKV-FBSDEs arising from the stochastic maximum principle. Crucially, the absence of volatility control ensures that the resulting FBODEs do not depend on the martingale term Z of the BSDE. In contrast, we consider a specific type of LQ-MFGs that satisfy the monotonicity condition, establishing both the unique existence of an MFE and convergence results. This setting allows for dependence on the control distribution within the state process and incorporates volatility control. However, unlike [9, 25], we do not derive the closed-loop equilibrium to align with our main focus.

texts.text: 1.2 Structure of this paper

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The paper is organized as follows. Section 2 formulates the N -player game and the corresponding mean field game, and states the main convergence result, Theorem 2.14. Section 3 characterizes the NE and the MFE via the associated classical and McKean-Vlasov FBSDE systems. Section 4 presents the proof of the main theorem. Section 5 analyzes a linear-quadratic example. Appendix A establishes the well-posedness of conditional MKV-FBSDEs using the method of continuation, guaranteeing the unique existence of the MFE. Appendix B constructs a conditionally i.i.d. sequence of solutions to the conditional MKV-FBSDEs, which serves as a prerequisite for the coupling technique used in the convergence analysis.

Section 3 characterizes the NE and the MFE via the associated classical and McKean-Vlasov FBSDE systems. Section 4 presents the proof of the main theorem. Section 5 analyzes a linear-quadratic example. Appendix A establishes the well-posedness of conditional MKV-FBSDEs using the method of continuation, guaranteeing the unique existence of the MFE. Appendix B constructs a conditionally i.i.d. sequence of solutions to the conditional MKV-FBSDEs, which serves as a prerequisite for the coupling technique used in the convergence analysis.

texts.orig: Remark 2.5. Let us elaborate on the assumptions. (i) is a prerequisite for applying the SMP. Specifically, the derivatives assumed here drive the adjoint equations in the FBSDEs that characterize the equilibria. (ii), (iii), and (v) are invoked repeatedly throughout the proof of the main theorem to establish moment bounds and error estimates for the FBSDE systems. (iv) ensures that the cost functional remains finite for any choice of admissible strategies. (vi) is usually referred to as the separability condition. Although this is restrictive, it is fairly standard in the literature on extended MFGs; see [11, 14, 19, 36]. In our setting, this condition decouples the optimizer Λ of the Hamiltonian (see Lemma 2.8) from the distribution of the control. In order to dispense with this condition, one might rely on an analysis of the fixed-point maps as detailed in [29, Section 3]. However, to avoid further technical complexity, we impose the condition.

[16, Theorem 2.14]; see (4.19) at the end of the proof of Theorem 2.14. If the solution Θ = ( X,Y,Z,Z 0 ) to the FBSDE (3.3) possesses a finite q -th moment E [ | Θ t | q ] < ∞ for some q > 2, we could effectively dispense with the smoothness assumption and instead employ the result by Fournier and Guillin [22, Theorem 1]. However, obtaining pointwise a priori estimates such as E [ | Z t | q ] < ∞ and E [ | Z 0 t | q ] < ∞ for the backward components is typically difficult. Even assuming E [sup t ∈ [0 ,T ] | X t | 2 ] < ∞ (which is itself subtle in our setting; see [30, Remark 5]), standard a priori estimates for FBSDEs typically yield bounds only on the integrated terms, such as E [( ∫ T 0 | Z t | 2 dt ) q ] and E [( ∫ T 0 | Z 0 t | 2 dt ) q ]. For this reason, we adopt this smoothness assumption.

Even assuming E [sup t ∈ [0 ,T ] | X t | 2 ] < ∞ (which is itself subtle in our setting; see [30, Remark 5]), standard a priori estimates for FBSDEs typically yield bounds only on the integrated terms, such as E [( ∫ T 0 | Z t | 2 dt ) q ] and E [( ∫ T 0 | Z 0 t | 2 dt ) q ]. For this reason, we adopt this smoothness assumption.

texts.orig: Unlike [25], this setting allows the state process to depend on the distribution of the control. The constants c 1 and c 2 characterize the nature of this interaction.
